serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken katsu curry rice served in a Singapore hawker or food court context typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, one or two pieces of breaded deep-fried chicken cutlet, and Japanese-style curry sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: 552D Chicken Katsu Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '552D Chicken Katsu Curry Rice(D)' with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken katsu curry rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked white jasmine rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The curry sauce and protein components moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain rice. A conservative mid-range estimate of 65 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 25.19 × 4.0) / 100 ≈ 65.5 for a 400 g serving (carbohydrate ~100.8 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g supporting DASH diet criteria.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium curry roux, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and limit portion size to 300 g or less to keep sodium intake below 700 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~65) combined with refined carbohydrate from panko breading delivers approximately 100 g of carbohydrate per standard 400 g serving, posing a significant postprandial glucose challenge.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycaemic impact, request baked or grilled chicken instead of deep-fried katsu, and limit the serving to no more than 250 g to reduce carbohydrate load to approximately 63 g per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying in oil raises total fat to 4.21 g/100 g with unknown saturated fat content; the breaded cutlet preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and increases dietary fat density relative to grilled preparations.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request baked or grilled chicken to reduce fat and AGE intake, choose a smaller portion of rice, and confirm the curry roux brand to avoid high saturated fat or trans fat formulations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines; nightshade vegetables (potato) in the curry may trigger individual sensitivity in some RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations entirely and request baked or grilled chicken with curry sauce and brown rice as a suitable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Katsu Curry Rice is a Japanese-origin dish widely served in Singapore food courts and hawker centres. It consists of steamed white rice topped with Japanese-style curry sauce and one or two pieces of chicken katsu — boneless chicken breast or thigh coated in panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The curry sauce is typically prepared from a Japanese curry roux block (such as S&B or House Foods brand) simmered with onion, potato, and carrot, producing a mild, slightly sweet, thick brown sauce that is distinctly different from Indian or Southeast Asian curries. The dish delivers a satisfying combination of textures: crispy breaded chicken, soft steamed rice, and smooth curry sauce. Per 100 g, it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre. The sodium content of 231 mg/100 g is moderate but accumulates significantly in a full 400 g serving (~924 mg). The dish is not suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its high-GI white rice base and refined carbohydrate from panko breading. It is also unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the deep-frying preparation method generating AGEs. For hypertension, it is borderline acceptable if portion-controlled and low-sodium roux is used. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Japanese food court stalls and is commonly associated with Japanese casual dining culture adapted for the local Singaporean market.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for an exact named entry '552D Chicken Katsu Curry Rice(D)'. All core macros and key micronutrients are present from this canonical approved source. GI is estimated from analogous white rice data from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '552D Chicken Katsu Curry Rice(D)' as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database record. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white jasmine rice (GI 64–72, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for chicken katsu curry rice as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. Halal status is not confirmed as Japanese curry roux brands vary; consumers should verify roux brand halal certification. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine adapted for the Singapore food court context.
